name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُتِلَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَآ أَكْفَرَهُۥ (١٧)
```

```english
Perish man! How ungrateful is he! (17)
```

```arabic
مِنْ أَىِّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقَهُۥ (١٨)
```

```english
From what did He create him? (18)
```

```arabic
مِن نُّطْفَةٍ خَلَقَهُۥ فَقَدَّرَهُۥ (١٩)
```

```english
He created him from a drop of [seminal] fluid; then proportioned him. (19)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ ٱلسَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُۥ (٢٠)
```

```english
Then He made the way easy for him; (20)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ أَمَاتَهُۥ فَأَقْبَرَهُۥ (٢١)
```

```english
then He made him die and buried him; (21)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ إِذَا شَآءَ أَنشَرَهُۥ (٢٢)
```

```english
and then, when He wished, resurrected him. (22)
```

```arabic
كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ مَآ أَمَرَهُۥ (٢٣)
```

```english
No indeed! He has not yet carried out what He has commanded him. (23)
```

```arabic
فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِلَىٰ طَعَامِهِۦٓ (٢٤)
```

```english
Let man consider his food: (24)
```

```arabic
أَنَّا صَبَبْنَا ٱلْمَآءَ صَبًّا (٢٥)
```

```english
We pour down plenteous water [from the sky], (25)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ شَقَقْنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ شَقًّا (٢٦)
```

```english
then We split the earth making fissures in it (26)
```

```arabic
فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا حَبًّا (٢٧)
```

```english
and make the grain grow in it, (27)
```

```arabic
وَعِنَبًا وَقَضْبًا (٢٨)
```

```english
as well as vines and vegetables, (28)
```

```arabic
وَزَيْتُونًا وَنَخْلًا (٢٩)
```

```english
olives and date palms, (29)
```

```arabic
وَحَدَآئِقَ غُلْبًا (٣٠)
```

```english
and densely-planted gardens, (30)
```

```arabic
وَفَـٰكِهَةً وَأَبًّا (٣١)
```

```english
fruits and pastures, (31)
```

```arabic
مَّتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَـٰمِكُمْ (٣٢)
```

```english
as a sustenance for you and your livestock. (32)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا جَآءَتِ ٱلصَّآخَّةُ (٣٣)
```

```english
So when the deafening Cry comes— (33)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يَفِرُّ ٱلْمَرْءُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ (٣٤)
```

```english
the day when a man will evade his brother, (34)
```

```arabic
وَأُمِّهِۦ وَأَبِيهِ (٣٥)
```

```english
his mother and his father, (35)
```

```arabic
وَصَـٰحِبَتِهِۦ وَبَنِيهِ (٣٦)
```

```english
his spouse and his sons— (36)
```

```arabic
لِكُلِّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ (٣٧)
```

```english
each of them will have a task to keep him preoccupied on that day. (37)
```

```arabic
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُّسْفِرَةٌ (٣٨)
```

```english
Some faces will be bright on that day, (38)
```

```arabic
ضَاحِكَةٌ مُّسْتَبْشِرَةٌ (٣٩)
```

```english
laughing and joyous, (39)
```

```arabic
وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَلَيْهَا غَبَرَةٌ (٤٠)
```

```english
and some faces on that day will be covered with dust, (40)
```

```arabic
تَرْهَقُهَا قَتَرَةٌ (٤١)
```

```english
overcast with gloom. (41)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَفَرَةُ ٱلْفَجَرَةُ (٤٢)
```

```english
It is they who are the faithless, the vicious. (42)
```

## (Commentary)

This is an invocation against man and an expression of wonder at his excessive disbelief (الكفر) in the Lordship (الربوبية) of his Lord. It points toward his affair in both inception and subsistence; for he possesses nothing for himself in terms of creation or governance. Rather, Allah, the Glorified, is the One who created him from a lowly drop of fluid (نطفة), then proportioned him, then eased the way for him, then caused him to die and provided him a grave, then, when He wills, He will resurrect him. Thus, He, the Glorified, is his Lord, his Creator, and the absolute Governor of his affairs. Yet, throughout the span of his existence, man does not fulfill what his Lord has commanded him, nor does he seek guidance through His guidance.

If man were to look only at his food—which is but one manifestation of His governance and a single handful from the oceans of His mercy—he would see such vastness of management and subtlety of craft as would dazzle his intellect and bewilder his mind. Beyond that are blessings that cannot be counted—*“And if you should count the favor of Allah, you could not enumerate them.”*

Thus, his concealment of his Lord’s governance and his abandonment of gratitude for His blessing is wondrous. Indeed, man is most unjust and ungrateful. They shall soon see the consequence of their gratitude or their disbelief in the form of joy and glad tidings, or dejection and the blackening of faces.

The verses—as you can see—do not resist being connected to what preceded them in a single context, even if some have said they were revealed for another reason, as will be mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ ما أَكْفَرَهُ** — *Perish man! How ungrateful is he! (80:17)* This is an invocation against man because of his natural tendency to immerse himself in following whims, forgetting the Lordship of his Lord, and waxing proud against following His commands.

His saying, **ما أَكْفَرَهُ** (How ungrateful is he!), is an expression of wonder at his excess in disbelief and his concealment of the manifest truth, while he sees that he is governed by the governance of Allah and possesses nothing of the management of his affairs besides Him, the Exalted.

The intended meaning of disbelief (الكفر) here is the absolute concealment of truth (ستر الحق), which applies to the denial of Lordship and the abandonment of worship (العبادة). This is supported by the end of the verse, which points to aspects of Divine governance (التدبير الربوبي) that correspond to disbelief in the sense of concealing the truth and abandoning worship. Some have interpreted disbelief (الكفر) here as the abandonment of gratitude and ungratefulness for blessings; while this is a correct meaning in itself, the previous meaning is more appropriate given the context.

The author of *al-Kashshaf* said: “**قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ** (Perish man!) is an invocation against him, and it is among the most hideous of their invocations because killing is the ultimate hardship and horror of this world. **ما أَكْفَرَهُ** (How ungrateful is he!) is wonder at his excess in being ungrateful for the blessing of Allah. You will not see a style more severe than this, nor harsher in touch, nor more indicative of wrath, nor further reaching in blame despite its brevity, nor more comprehensive of all rebukes within such a short text.” End of quote.

It has also been said that the sentence **ما أَكْفَرَهُ** is interrogative, meaning: "What is it that made him a disbeliever?" However, the previous view is more eloquent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مِنْ أَيِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ** — *From what stuff did He create him? (80:18)* Its meaning, as dictated by the context, is: From what thing did Allah create man such that it would be right for him to transgress and wax proud against faith and obedience? The subject (الفاعل) of His saying **خَلَقَهُ** (He created him) and the subsequent verbs is omitted to signal its obviousness; for it is known by the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and even the polytheists admitted that there is no creator but Allah, the Exalted.

The interrogation serves to emphasize the wonder expressed in His saying **ما أَكْفَرَهُ**; wonder only occurs regarding events (الحوادث) whose cause is not apparent. Thus, it was first stated that man’s excess in disbelief is wondrous, then it was asked: Is there anything in his creation, when Allah created him, that necessitates this excess in disbelief? The answer follows by negating this, showing he has no argument to lean on nor any excuse to offer. For he was created from a lowly fluid, possessing nothing of his own creation, nor the governance of his affairs in his life, his death, or his resurrection. In short, the interrogation is a preamble to the answer found in His saying: **مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ خَلَقَهُ** (From a drop of fluid He created him), etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ خَلَقَهُ فَقَدَّرَهُ** — *From a [lowly] drop of fluid He created him, and then proportioned him. (80:19)* The indefiniteness of "drop of fluid" (نطفة) is for belittlement; meaning, from a lowly, insignificant drop of fluid He created him. Thus, it is not right for him, given this origin, to transgress in his disbelief and wax proud against obedience.

His saying **فَقَدَّرَهُ** (and then proportioned him) means He gave him his measure (القدر) in his essence (الذات), attributes, and actions. Thus, he has no right to exceed the stage (الطور) proportioned for him or overstep the limit defined for him. Divine governance has encompassed him from every side; he has no independence to attain what has not been proportioned for him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ** — *Then He eased the way for him. (80:20)* The apparent context, intended to negate any excuse for man in his disbelief and pride, is that the "way" (السبيل)—mentioned here in absolute terms—refers to the way toward obedience to Allah and compliance with His commands. If you wish, you may say: the way toward goodness and felicity (السعادة).

Thus, the verse serves to repel a potential objection (دفع الدخل). For when it is said, "From a drop of fluid He created him, and then proportioned him," a listener might imagine that if creation and proportioning encompass man from every side, then man's actions—stemming from his essence and attributes—are proportioned, written, and linked to the Divine Will (المشية الربوبية) which never fails. Consequently, man's actions would be of necessary occurrence (ضرورية الثبوت) and mandatory realization, with man being coerced (مجبر) into them and lacking free will (الاختيار). In that case, man would have no hand in his disbelief if he disbelieves, nor in his wickedness if he sins, nor in failing to fulfill what Allah commanded him; rather, that would be by His decree (التقدير) and will. Thus, no blame or reproach would attach to man, nor would any religious call apply to him, as all of that is predicated on free will, and [in this hypothetical] there is no free will.

So, He repelled this doubt by His saying: **ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ** (Then He eased the way for him). The gist of it is that creation and proportioning do not contradict man being an agent of free will (مختار) in what he is commanded of faith and obedience. He has a path to the felicity for which he was created; thus, "everyone is eased toward that for which he was created." This is because the proportioning (التقدير) applies to human actions through the channel of his free will. The Divine Will is linked to man doing such-and-such by his own will and choice. Thus, the action originates from man by his choice, and insofar as it is volitional, it is subject to proportioning.

Therefore, man is a free agent in his action and responsible for it, even if it is subject to the decree (القدر). We have repeatedly discussed this concept in this book under the verses appropriate to it.

It has also been said that "easing the way" refers to facilitating man's exit from his mother's womb; meaning, He then eased for man the way of exit while he was a fetus created from a drop of fluid.

It has also been said that it refers to guidance (الهداية) to the religion and the clarification of the path of good and evil, as He said: *“And showed him the two paths” (90:10)*. However, the first view is more appropriate.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ أَماتَهُ فَأَقْبَرَهُ** — *Then He caused him to die and provided him a grave. (80:21)* "Causing to die" (الإماتة) is the bringing of death upon man. "Providing a grave" (الإقبار) refers to burying him in a grave and hiding him in the belly of the earth. This is based on the general custom that people have followed. By this association, it is attributed to Him, the Exalted, because He is the One who guided them to that and inspired them with it. Thus, the act has an attribution to Him just as it has an attribution to people.

It has also been said that "providing a grave" means making him "one who possesses a grave." The meaning of making him possess a grave is His command to bury him as an honor to him, so that his corpse is hidden and people are not harmed by it or repulsed.

The first view is more suited to the context of the verses, which are set forth to remind of His creative governance (التدبير التكويني) of man, rather than the legislative governance (التدبير التشريعي) upon which this second view is based.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ إِذا شاءَ أَنْشَرَهُ** — *Then, when He wishes, He will resurrect him. (80:22)* In *al-Majma‘*: "Resurrection (الإنشار) is the bringing to life for activity after death, like the unfolding (نشر) of a garment after it was folded." End of quote. Thus, it refers to the Resurrection (البعث) when Allah wills. In it is an indication that it occurs suddenly, known to none but Him, the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا لَمَّا يَقْضِ ما أَمَرَهُ** — *No indeed! He has not yet unfulfilled what He commanded him. (80:23)* What the context provides is that **كَلَّا** (No indeed!) is a rebuff (ردع) of the implication of a question invited by the context and hinted at by His saying: **لَمَّا يَقْضِ ما أَمَرَهُ** (He has not yet fulfilled what He commanded him). It is as if, when it was pointed out that man is a creature governed by Him, the Exalted, from the beginning of his existence to its end—in terms of creation, proportioning, easing the way, causing death, providing a grave, and resurrection, all of which are blessings from Him—it was asked: "So what has man done in this state? Has he submitted to Lordship, or has he thanked the blessing?" The answer was given: "No indeed!" Then it was clarified: "He has not yet fulfilled what Allah commanded him; rather, he disbelieved and disobeyed."

It has become clear from the preceding that the pronoun in **يَقْضِ** (he fulfills) refers to man, and the meaning of his "fulfilling" is his performing what Allah commanded him. It has been said that the pronoun refers to Allah, the Exalted, and the meaning is: "Allah has not yet decreed (يَقْضِ) for this disbeliever to perform what He commanded him of faith and obedience; rather, He only commanded him with what He commanded to complete the proof (إتمام الحجة) against him." This is far-fetched.

It has also become clear that the blame and reproach in the verses are directed at man due to his natural inclination toward excess in disbelief, as in His saying: *“Indeed man is most unjust and ungrateful” (14:34)*. Thus, it applies to those who clothe themselves in disbelief and exceed in it through obstinacy. From this, the lack of soundness in what was narrated from some—that the verse is general for both the disbeliever and the Muslim, as no one has worshipped Him with the worship He truly deserves—becomes apparent.

This is because the pronoun refers to the "man" mentioned at the beginning of the verses, characterized by his natural urge toward excess in disbelief, and it applies to those who are actually characterized by it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلْيَنْظُرِ الْإِنْسانُ إِلى طَعامِهِ** — *Let man consider his food. (80:24)* This is branched (متفرع) from what preceded, like the branching of detail from a summary. In it is a direction for man to look at his food, which he consumes and from which he draws strength for his survival. It is but one of the countless things prepared by Divine governance to meet his needs in life, so that he may contemplate it and witness the vastness of Divine governance that dazzles his mind and bewilders his intellect, and how Divine care—in its precision and encompassment—is attached to the well-being of his state and the rectitude of his affairs.

The "man" intended here—as has been said—is other than the "man" mentioned previously in His saying: **قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ ما أَكْفَرَهُ** (Perish man! How ungrateful is he!). For the former refers specifically to the man who is excessive in disbelief, whereas the "man" mentioned in this verse, who is commanded to look, is general, encompassing every human being. For this reason, he was mentioned explicitly (أظهر) rather than by a pronoun.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَنَّا صَبَبْنَا الْماءَ صَبًّا** — *We poured down water plenteously (80:25)* — down to His saying — **وَلِأَنْعامِكُمْ** — *and for your livestock. (80:32)* The common recitation is **أَنَّا** with the *fathah* on the *hamzah*, and it is a detailed explanation of His governance of man's food. Yes, it is an initial stage of detail; as for a discourse that fully accounts for the specifics of the system that prepared these matters, the vast system running through each of them, and the cosmic links (الروابط الكونية) between each of them and man, that is something beyond the usual scope of explanation.

In short, His saying: **أَنَّا صَبَبْنَا الْماءَ صَبًّا** (We poured down water plenteously). "Pouring" (الصب) is the spilling of water from above. What is meant by pouring water is the sending down of rains upon the earth to grow plants. It is not unlikely that it includes the flowing of springs and rivers, for the water reserves in the belly of the earth are only formed from rain.

His saying: **ثُمَّ شَقَقْنَا الْأَرْضَ شَقًّا** — *then We split the earth into fissures (80:26)*. Its apparent meaning is the splitting of the earth by the plants emerging from it. For this reason, it was joined to the pouring of water with **ثُمَّ** (then), and the growing of grain was joined to it with **فَ** (so/then).

His saying: **فَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها حَبًّا** — *and made the grain grow in it (80:27)*. The pronoun in **فِيها** (in it) refers to the earth. "Grain" (الحب) refers to the genus of grain that man consumes, such as wheat, barley, and the like. The same applies to grapes, clover, and others.

His saying: **وَعِنَباً وَقَضْباً** — *and vines and clover (80:28)*. Grapes (العنب) are well-known; the term is also applied to the grapevine, and perhaps that is what is intended in the verse, similar to the olive tree.

*Qadb* (القضب) is the fresh, succulent greens that man eats; it is called *qadb* because it is cut (يُقضب) time after time. It has also been said that it is what is cut from plants to be used as fodder for animals.

His saying: **وَزَيْتُوناً وَنَخْلاً** — *and olives and palms (80:29)*. Both are well-known.

His saying: **وَحَدائِقَ غُلْباً** — *and densely-planted gardens (80:30)*. *Hada’iq* (الحدائق) is the plural of *hadiqah*, which is defined as a walled garden. *Ghulb* (الغلب) is the plural of *ghalba’*; it is said: "a *ghalba’* tree," meaning a massive, thick tree. Thus, *hada’iq ghulb* are gardens containing massive, thick trees.

His saying: **وَفاكِهَةً وَأَبًّا** — *and fruits and pasturage (80:31)*. It is said that *fakiha* (fruit) refers to fruits in general; others say it refers to everything except grapes and pomegranates. It is said that the specific mention of what falls under "fruit" first, like olives and palms, is due to their importance. *Abb* (الأب) is herbage and pasture.

His saying: **مَتاعاً لَكُمْ وَلِأَنْعامِكُمْ** — *as a provision for you and for your livestock. (80:32)* This is an object of purpose (مفعول له); meaning, We grew what We grew of that which you eat so that it may be a source of enjoyment for you and for the livestock you have designated for yourselves.

The shift (الالتفات) from the third person to the second person in the verse is to emphasize the bestowal of favor through governance or the granting of blessings.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِذا جاءَتِ الصَّاخَّةُ** — *So when the Deafening Cry comes (80:33)*. This is an indication of the ultimate end of the aforementioned general Divine governance of man, insofar as it involves a Divine command of servitude (العبودية) which man either fulfills or does not. That is the Day of Resurrection, in which man is paid the full recompense for his deeds.

The *Sakhkhah* (الصاخة) is the piercing cry that deafens the ears by its intensity; what is meant by it is the blast of the Trumpet.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ يَفِرُّ الْمَرْءُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ وَأُمِّهِ وَأَبِيهِ وَصاحِبَتِهِ وَبَنِيهِ** — *the day when a man will evade his brother, and his mother and his father, and his wife and his sons (80:34-36)*. This is an indication of the severity of that day. Those who were counted as man's closest kin and associates—those to whom he would take recourse, with whom he found comfort, and whom he took as supporters and helpers, seeking refuge with them in the world—he will flee from them on the Day of Resurrection. This is because the severity will have encompassed him such that it does not allow him to occupy himself with anyone else or care for anyone besides himself, whoever they may be. When distress becomes great, intense, and looms over man, it pulls him toward itself and diverts him from everything else.

The evidence for this meaning is His subsequent saying: **لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ** — *that day every one of them will have a state to keep him occupied. (80:37)* That is, it suffices to keep him from being occupied with anyone else.

Other reasons have been suggested for man's flight from his relatives and associates on that day, but there is no evidence for them, so we have refrained from mentioning them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ مُسْفِرَةٌ ضاحِكَةٌ مُسْتَبْشِرَةٌ** — *Some faces on that day will be bright, laughing, [and] joyous. (80:38-39)* This is an explanation of the division of people on that day into two groups: the people of felicity and the people of wretchedness. It indicates that they are known by their marks (سيماهم) in their faces. The "brightening" (إسفار) of the face is its radiance and illumination due to joy and happiness. Its being "joyous" (استبشار) is its beaming upon witnessing that which contains glad tidings.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَلَيْها غَبَرَةٌ** — *And some faces on that day will be covered with dust (80:40)*. This is dust and gloom, which is the mark of anxiety and grief.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَرْهَقُها قَتَرَةٌ** — *overcast by soot. (80:41)* That is, blackness and darkness will overcome and cover them. The state of the two groups has been explained in these four verses by describing the state of their faces, because the face is the mirror of the heart in its joy and its sorrow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكَفَرَةُ الْفَجَرَةُ** — *It is they who are the faithless, the vicious. (80:42)* Meaning, those who combine disbelief (الكفر) in belief and wickedness (الفجور)—which is heinous sin—in action. Or, those who are ungrateful for Allah's blessings and are wicked. This is a definition of the second group, the people of wretchedness. He did not provide a similar definition for the first group, the people of felicity, because the discourse is directed toward warning and concern with the state of the people of wretchedness.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from ‘Ikrimah regarding His saying: **قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ ما أَكْفَرَهُ** (Perish man! How ungrateful is he!). He said: "It was revealed concerning ‘Utbah ibn Abi Lahab when he said: 'I disbelieve in the Lord of the Star when it goes down.' So the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم invoked against him, and a lion took him on the road to Syria."

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from Amir al-Mu'minin عليه السلام in a long hadith: "**قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ ما أَكْفَرَهُ** means: cursed is man."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding **ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ** (Then He eased the way for him), he said: "He eased for him the path of goodness."

**I say:** What is meant by it is that He made him a free agent in his actions, by which his treading the path of felicity and his reaching the perfection for which he was created is made easy. Thus, the report corresponds to the view we previously offered in the commentary of the verse.

In the same source, regarding His saying **وَقَضْباً** (and clover), he said: "*Qadb* is alfalfa."

In the same source, regarding His saying **وَفاكِهَةً وَأَبًّا** (and fruits and pasturage), he said: "*Abb* is grass for the cattle."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu ‘Ubayd narrated in his *Fada’il* from Ibrahim al-Taymi, who said: "Abu Bakr al-Siddiq was asked about His saying **وَأَبًّا**, and he said: 'Which sky would shade me and which earth would carry me if I were to say regarding the Book of Allah that which I do not know?'"

In the same source, it is narrated by Sa‘id ibn Mansur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Sa‘d, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in *Shu‘ab al-Iman*, al-Khatib, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—from Anas, that ‘Umar read on the pulpit: **فَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها حَبًّا وَعِنَباً وَقَضْباً** — down to His saying — **وَأَبًّا**. He said: "All of this we have known, but what is *al-abb*?" Then he cast away a staff that was in his hand and said: "This, by the life of Allah, is affectation. What does it matter to you if you do not know what *al-abb* is? Follow what has been clarified for you of its guidance from the Book and act upon it—and what you do not recognize, leave it to its Lord."

In the same source, ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated from ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn Yazid that a man asked ‘Umar about His saying **وَأَبًّا**, and when he saw them [discussing it], he approached them with a whip.

**I say:** This is based on their prohibition of researching the knowledge of the Book, even the explanation of its words.

In *al-Irshad* by al-Mufid, it is narrated: "Abu Bakr was asked about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **وَفاكِهَةً وَأَبًّا**, and he did not know the meaning of *al-abb* from the Quran. He said: 'Which sky would shade me or which earth would carry me—or what shall I do if I say regarding the Book of Allah that which I do not know? As for fruit (الفاكهة), we know it; as for *al-abb*, Allah knows best.'"

This reached Amir al-Mu'minin عليه السلام, and he said: "Glory be to Allah! Did he not know that *al-abb* is herbage and pasture? And that His saying, the Exalted: **وَفاكِهَةً وَأَبًّا** is a counting by Allah of His blessings upon His creation in what He nourished them with and created for them and for their livestock, by which their souls live and their bodies are sustained."

In *al-Majma‘*, it is narrated from ‘Ata’ ibn Yasar from Sawdah, the wife of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: 'People will be resurrected barefoot, naked, and uncircumcised; sweat will bridle them and reach the lobes of the ears.' She said: 'I said: O Messenger of Allah, what a shame! Will we look at one another when that comes?' He said: 'People will be too occupied for that,' and the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم recited: **لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ** (that day every one of them will have a state to keep him occupied).'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying: **لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ**, he said: "An occupation that busies him from anyone else."
